title: TPU v1 · Process monitoring and PCM (process control monitor): fabrication is not “finish first, inspect later,” but a continuous “measure as you build” discipline—inline metrology checks film thickness and linewidth, defect inspection scans for particles, and small PCM test structures in the scribe lines measure actual 28 nm transistor parameters; all data feed statistical process control (SPC) to determine whether a lot is “in control” or “drifting.” When drift appears, the line stops for corrective action before the next operation, instead of discovering only after an entire batch of large dies containing the 256×256 array has been ruined
oneLine: This case focuses on “process monitoring and PCM.” Chip fabrication involves hundreds of irreversible operations over weeks or months; if testing occurs only at the end, an intermediate process drift can scrap an entire wafer lot. Manufacturing therefore “measures as it builds”: inline metrology measures film thickness, critical dimension (CD), and overlay; defect inspection scans for particles and pattern defects; and process control monitor (PCM) structures place dedicated small test devices—test transistors, resistors, capacitors, ring oscillators, and others—in the scribe lines between dies. Probes then measure the real electrical parameters of the lot’s 28 nm transistors, including threshold voltage Vt, saturation current Idsat, sheet resistance Rs, and capacitance. All measurements enter SPC charts. Upper and lower control limits (UCL/LCL) evaluate statistical process stability: values within the limits with no nonrandom trend indicate an in-control process, while points beyond a limit or sustained one-sided trends indicate drift and trigger a line stop and corrective action. Engineering specification limits serve a different purpose: they determine whether measured output is acceptable for the product. A wafer lot may proceed only when the process is statistically in control and every required metrology and PCM measurement is within specification; satisfying either condition alone is insufficient. This catches deviations early and contains losses before the next operation, avoiding wholesale loss of TPU v1’s ≤331 mm² large dies. The discussion uses TPU’s known 256×256 systolic array, 24 MiB UB, 700 MHz, 28 nm process, and ≤331 mm² die only as the object under manufacturing control. Metrology, defect inspection, PCM structures, and SPC limits are standard process-control practices for this class of 28 nm large chip; TPU v1 does not disclose proprietary metrology, defect, PCM, or yield data.

legend.name: PCM test structures in the scribe line (yellow strips/squares on the wafer)
legend.desc: The “representative” of the process. A PCM (Process Control Monitor, also called a process-monitor structure, test structure, or drop-in) is not part of the TPU circuit. It is a small standardized structure placed deliberately in the narrow scribe line between dies—the lane that will later be cut away and consumes no die area. It contains test transistors, resistor bars, capacitors, ring oscillators, and other basic devices. Because these structures are fabricated on the same wafer and through the same operations as the real TPU die, measuring them reveals what the lot’s 28 nm devices actually look like. The key advantage is access to real process parameters without dismantling a TPU chip, using space that will be discarded during dicing.

legend.name: ① Inline metrology (film thickness/CD/overlay)
legend.desc: The process-control “ruler.” Between operations, nondestructive measurements track critical geometry: film thickness, critical dimension (CD, or linewidth—including whether wires in the 256×256 array meet target width), overlay error between layers, surface topography, and related metrics. These measurements answer whether patterns are correct and layers are accurately aligned. Sampling after each critical step catches deviations while rework may still be possible. Metrology complements PCM electrical tests: metrology checks geometry; PCM checks electrical behavior.

legend.name: ② Defect inspection (particles/pattern defects)
legend.desc: The process-control “fault finder.” Optical or electron-beam tools scan the wafer surface for random particles, scratches, and pattern defects such as shorts, opens, and bridges introduced by processing. The results quantify defect density, map spatial distributions, and identify defect hot spots. Random scatter suggests incidental contamination, whereas clusters or rings often indicate a systematic equipment or process issue that must be traced and corrected. Defects directly govern large-die yield, making TPU’s ≤331 mm² large die especially sensitive.

legend.name: ③ PCM process-control testing (probing 28 nm transistor parameters)
legend.desc: The process-control “electrical physical.” At suitable fabrication stages—often after FEOL and once metal enables probing—a probe card contacts PCM structures in the scribe line and electrically measures actual parameters of the lot’s 28 nm transistors and devices: threshold voltage Vt, saturation current Idsat, sheet resistance Rs, capacitance, leakage, ring-oscillator frequency, and more. These values reveal whether fabricated transistors are fast enough, whether they leak, and whether they meet specification. The division of labor is clear: inline metrology checks geometry, while PCM checks device electrical performance. Only in-spec PCM parameters justify treating the wafer’s TPU transistors as usable.

legend.name: ④ SPC statistical process control (control limits determine in-control/drift status)
legend.desc: The process-control “brain.” Measurements from metrology, inspection, and PCM are tracked over time on SPC (Statistical Process Control) charts, with upper and lower control limits (UCL/LCL) defined for each parameter and points plotted wafer by wafer or lot by lot. Points within the control limits and without sustained nonrandom trends indicate a statistically in-control process; they do not by themselves establish product acceptance. A point beyond a control limit, or several successive points moving in one direction even before crossing it, indicates process drift and triggers an alarm, line stop, root-cause analysis, and correction. Separately, engineering specification limits determine whether each measured result is acceptable. Release requires both conditions: the process is in control and all required measurements are within specification. SPC’s value is trend-based early warning: it can catch an emerging shift before it creates scrap and prevent lot-wide loss.

steps.cap: ① Inline metrology: nondestructively measure film thickness, critical-dimension CD (linewidths in the 256×256 array), and overlay between operations—checking whether patterns are correct and layers align accurately while geometric deviations can still be reworked

② Defect inspection: scan wafer surfaces for particles and pattern defects, quantify defect density, and locate hot spots. Random scatter suggests incidental contamination; clusters or rings point to systematic defect sources requiring root-cause analysis—defects directly consume large-die yield

③ PCM process monitoring: probe PCM test structures in the scribe line and electrically measure the lot’s actual 28 nm transistor parameters (Vt/Idsat/Rs/capacitance, etc.), obtaining process electrical performance without dismantling a TPU chip

④ Use SPC control limits to judge process stability and specification limits to judge acceptance: within UCL/LCL with no nonrandom trend → statistically in control; beyond a control limit or sustained trend → stop and correct. Release only when the process is in control and all required measurements are within specification

before: Define monitored parameters, specifications, and control limits: Before fabrication, specify exactly what to watch: film-thickness/CD/overlay budgets for critical layers, acceptable defect density, PCM measurements for 28 nm transistors (Vt, Idsat, Rs, capacitance, leakage, and others), their specification limits, and corresponding SPC upper/lower control limits (UCL/LCL). Without specifications and control limits, a measured value cannot be classified as normal variation or drift.

Place PCM test structures in the scribe lines: This must actually be done during tapeout/layout in ⑩. Standard PCM devices—test transistors, resistors, capacitors, ring oscillators, and others—are placed in the scribe lines between dies, where they will be cut away, and fabricated alongside the TPU die through the same operations. Poor placement or inaccessible probe pads would prevent measurement of the lot’s true device parameters.

Plan metrology and inspection sampling: Exhaustively measuring every point on every wafer is too slow and costly. Define which critical operations require measurement, how many wafers to sample per lot, and which PCM sites across each wafer—center, edge, and quadrants—to test. The plan must represent die-wide uniformity without overwhelming line throughput, balancing sufficient control against unnecessary measurement.

Calibrate metrology, inspection, and probing equipment: Metrology tools, defect scanners, and PCM probe stations must be calibrated and their repeatability confirmed. If the measuring instrument itself drifts, apparent “process drift” may be an equipment artifact. SPC points become trustworthy only after measurement-system error is separated from real process variation.
during: Core principle: “measure as you build”; never fabricate blindly and inspect only at the end. A chip passes through hundreds of irreversible operations over weeks or months. If the process shifts midway without detection, an entire wafer lot continues on a faulty foundation and may ultimately be scrapped. Process control exists to contain a problem before it produces lot-wide loss—the earlier it is caught, the smaller the damage.

Three complementary monitoring channels are required for complete coverage: Inline metrology checks geometry—film thickness, CD linewidth, and overlay; defect inspection checks for particles and pattern defects; PCM checks device electrical performance, including whether transistor Vt/Idsat/Rs values meet specification. Geometry alone can appear correct while transistors fail electrically; electrical tests alone can miss random particles striking a large die. None of the three is optional.

PCM is an ingenious way to “examine the process without dismantling a chip”: Standard test structures occupy scribe lines that will be cut away and therefore consume no die area. Because they are fabricated on the same wafer and through the same operations as the real TPU die, their measurements reveal what the lot’s 28 nm transistors, resistors, and capacitors actually look like—without sacrificing a TPU chip for destructive analysis.

SPC provides trend-based early warning, not an alarm only after scrap appears: Plot each parameter wafer by wafer or lot by lot. Control limits and specification limits answer different questions. Remaining within UCL/LCL with no nonrandom trend means the process is statistically in control; crossing a control limit or showing a sustained trend signals drift and triggers an alarm, line stop, and root-cause correction. Separately, specification limits determine whether the measured output is acceptable. Release requires both an in-control process and all required measurements within specification. This detects emerging shifts before they create scrap while preventing a stable but off-spec process from advancing.

Every wafer lot must “pass PCM before proceeding”: Fabrication is a relay. If front-end 28 nm transistor parameters have already drifted out of specification, spending additional weeks on metal interconnect and packaging only wastes more value. PCM must confirm each lot’s transistors are in specification before release to the next stage; failing lots are held for corrective action or scrapped, never advanced with a known problem.

Disclosure boundary: Inline measurements of film thickness/CD/overlay, defect inspection and density analysis, scribe-line PCM test structures, electrical PCM testing of transistor parameters, SPC control limits, and drift rules are standard process-control practices for this class of 28 nm large chip. TPU v1 does not disclose proprietary measurement items, defect density, PCM structures, transistor specifications, control limits, or yield. This section uses only TPU’s known 256×256 array, 24 MiB UB, 700 MHz, 28 nm process, and ≤331 mm² die as the object under manufacturing control. Numeric values and SPC curves in the figure are illustrative, not proprietary TPU manufacturing data.

result.label: Three-part monitoring system
result.value: Inline metrology (film thickness/CD/overlay) + defect inspection (particles/pattern defects) + PCM (scribe-line test structures measuring 28 nm transistor electrical parameters)—covering geometry, defects, and electrical behavior

result.label: What PCM measures
result.value: Probes on scribe-line PCM structures measure threshold voltage Vt, saturation current Idsat, sheet resistance Rs, capacitance, leakage, ring-oscillator frequency, and other device parameters, confirming that the lot’s 28 nm transistors meet specification

result.label: Stability and release decision
result.value: SPC control limits judge process stability: within UCL/LCL with no nonrandom trend → statistically in control; beyond a control limit or sustained trend → stop and correct. Specification limits separately judge product acceptance. Release requires both an in-control process and all required measurements within specification

result.label: Core value
result.value: Contain process drift “before the next operation,” limiting losses instead of discovering failure after an entire wafer lot is complete—especially important when scrapping a lot of ≤331 mm² large dies is extremely costly

result.label: Release criterion
result.value: Every wafer lot must use PCM to confirm transistor parameters are within specification before entering the next stage; failing lots are held for corrective action or scrapped and never advanced with a known issue

result.label: Disclosure boundary
result.value: Metrology/defect inspection/PCM/SPC are standard process-control practices for this class of 28 nm large chip; TPU v1 does not disclose proprietary measurement items, defect density, PCM structures, parameter specifications, control limits, or yield, and figure values are illustrative

after: Make an inline disposition: release or stop: After every critical operation, feed metrology, defect, and PCM data into SPC. First use control-chart behavior to judge process stability, then compare every required result with its engineering specification limits. Release the lot only when the process is in control and all required measurements are within specification. If drift or an out-of-spec result is detected, immediately stop or hold the lot, investigate and correct the cause, and resume only after verification—containing the loss and preventing spread to later lots.

Analyze trends and trace equipment/process causes: For sustained shifts or clustered defect hot spots, identify the tool, operation, or recipe causing the systematic issue, then tune parameters or service equipment. Random isolated defects are tracked through defect-density models. Lot-to-lot drift in PCM parameters likewise feeds back to process engineering for fine adjustment.

Gate entry to wafer-level electrical test (CP): Once PCM confirms that process-level device parameters meet specification, the fully fabricated wafer proceeds to wafer sort/CP, where each die is powered and functionally screened. PCM asks “is this lot’s process healthy?”; CP asks “does each die work?” Process monitoring establishes the foundation for downstream packaging/test and yield binning.

Retain traceable data: Archive each lot’s metrology, defect, PCM measurements, and SPC charts in the manufacturing process record as traceable process-control evidence for later yield analysis, customer audits, and optimization of subsequent lots. TPU’s specific monitoring data are not public; maintaining such records is standard practice.

Feed lessons back into process and design iteration: If a parameter repeatedly approaches a control limit or drifts, either tighten process control or improve manufacturability through design-side DFM and additional margin, then validate the change on the next lot. Process control, yield, and design form a continuous improvement loop.
source: In-Datacenter Performance Analysis of a TPU, Jouppi et al., ISCA 2017 (the paper discloses TPU v1’s known 256×256 8-bit MAC systolic array, 24 MiB Unified Buffer, 700 MHz, 28 nm process, and ≤331 mm² die, used here as the object under manufacturing control; inline metrology for film thickness/CD/overlay, defect inspection and defect density, scribe-line PCM test structures, PCM electrical measurements of transistor parameters such as Vt/Idsat/Rs/capacitance, SPC statistical process control, control limits, and drift rules are standard manufacturing/process-control practices for this class of 28 nm large chip; figure values, SPC curves, and parameter specifications are illustrative and are not public disclosures of TPU v1’s foundry or proprietary metrology, defect, PCM, or yield data)
